perturbation.original_text: 5.1 If the Recipient fails to submit an offer, he must then return all documents provided by the GSA within two months following the deadline for the submission of offers.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 If the Recipient fails to submit an offer, he must then return all documents provided by the GSA within six months following the deadline for the submission of offers.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the document return deadline to six months contradicts standard contractual expectations of promptness and efficiency, potentially violating implied duties of good faith and fair dealing under contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Principles of Contract Law - Duty of Good Faith
perturbation.law_explanation: While the UCC primarily governs the sale of goods, its principle of good faith is widely recognized and applied across various contract law domains, including confidentiality agreements. Extending the return period to six months could be interpreted as acting in bad faith, especially if it hinders the GSA's ability to proceed with the procurement process efficiently. Good faith requires honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing. A six-month delay could be seen as unreasonable and detrimental to the GSA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: and Uniform Warehouse Receipts Act in 1906; the Uniform Bills of Lading Act and Uniform Stock Transfer Act in 1909; and the Uniform Conditional Sales Act in 1918. The ULC officially took on the task of drafting a comprehensive code to provide guidelines for all commercial transactions in 1940. In 1942, the ULC and the American Law Institute joined in a partnership that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the document return deadline from two to six months, potentially conflicting with the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 1-304, states: "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." The Law Explanation suggests that a six-month delay "could be interpreted as acting in bad faith," especially if it hinders the GSA's ability to proceed with the procurement process efficiently. The change weakens the expectation of promptness, creating an inconsistency regarding the reasonable execution of contractual duties, therefore creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7.2 The obligations imposed by Articles 2 and 3 above with respect to the access to, use, distribution, disclosure and protection of Confidential Information for pursuing the Purpose shall apply:  for ten years with regards to Confidential Information;
perturbation.changed_text: 7.2 The obligations imposed by Articles 2 and 3 above with respect to the access to, use, distribution, disclosure and protection of Confidential Information for pursuing the Purpose shall apply:  for one year with regards to Confidential Information;
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: EU Directive 2016/943 on the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets)
perturbation.law_explanation: EU Directive 2016/943 aims to harmonize the protection of trade secrets across the EU. While it doesn't specify a minimum protection period, Article 7 requires member states to ensure that legal proceedings for trade secret misappropriation can be brought within a reasonable time. A one-year confidentiality period might be deemed insufficient to allow for the discovery and prosecution of misappropriation, especially for complex technologies. The directive aims to deter unfair competition, and a short confidentiality period undermines this objective. The Berne Convention states that literary and artistic works shall be protected for the life of the author and 50 years after his death.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from ten years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation states this may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws. The Law Explanation references EU Directive 2016/943, which aims to protect trade secrets. While it doesn't specify a minimum protection period, Article 7 requires that legal proceedings can be brought within a reasonable time. A one-year confidentiality period might be deemed insufficient to allow for the discovery and prosecution of misappropriation, undermining the directive's objective to deter unfair competition. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of Directive (EU) 2016/943 regarding the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets) against their unlawful acquisition, use and disclosure. Therefore, the reduced confidentiality period creates a legal contradiction by potentially undermining the protections afforded to trade secrets under EU law. As such, the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition is met.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 If the Recipient submits an offer but is not awarded the contract, he must return all documents provided by the GSA within two months following publication of the award notice.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 If the Recipient submits an offer but is not awarded the contract, he is not required to return any documents provided by the GSA.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to return confidential documents contradicts standard confidentiality practices and increases the risk of unauthorized use or disclosure, potentially violating data protection laws if the documents contain personal data.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(1)(e) of the GDPR requires that personal data be kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed. If the Recipient retains documents containing personal data indefinitely after not being awarded the contract, it violates this principle of storage limitation. The GSA has a duty to ensure that personal data is not kept longer than necessary, and waiving the return requirement undermines this obligation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’). The controller shall be responsible for, and be able to demonstrate compliance with, paragraph 1 (‘accountability’). Suitable Recitals ( 39 ) Principles of Data Processing ( 74 ) Responsibility and Liability of the Controller ← Art. 4 GDPR Art. 6 GDPR → GDPR Table of contents Report error
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement to return documents, which directly contradicts the principle of storage limitation as outlined in Article 5 of the GDPR. The original text ensures that documents are returned within two months if the recipient is not awarded the contract, aligning with the principle of not keeping data longer than necessary. The changed text, by removing this obligation, allows the recipient to retain documents indefinitely, potentially violating GDPR's storage limitation principle, especially if the documents contain personal data. The law snippet states: 'kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed', emphasizing the contradiction introduced.

perturbation.original_text: 4.5 is disclosed pursuant to the request of a governmental or jurisdictional authority or is disclosed according to the law or regulations of any country with jurisdiction over the Recipient; in either case the Recipient, subject to possible constraints of such governmental or jurisdictional authority, shall immediately give the GSA a written notice of the above request and shall reasonably cooperate with the GSA in order to avoid or limit such disclosure;
perturbation.changed_text: 4.5 is disclosed pursuant to the request of a governmental or jurisdictional authority or is disclosed according to the law or regulations of any country with jurisdiction over the Recipient; in such case the Recipient has no obligation to notify the GSA.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the obligation to notify the GSA about disclosures requested by governmental authorities conflicts with the principle of cooperation and transparency, potentially violating legal obligations related to national security or data protection, especially if the confidential information is classified.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Council Decision 2013/488/EU on the security rules for protecting EU classified information
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5(3) of Council Decision 2013/488/EU outlines security principles for protecting EU classified information. While it doesn't explicitly mandate notification in all disclosure cases, the overall spirit emphasizes the need for a 'need-to-know' principle and secure handling. Removing the notification obligation hinders the GSA's ability to assess the legitimacy and scope of the disclosure request and to take appropriate security measures. This lack of transparency can compromise the protection of classified information, potentially violating the decision's intent.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires the recipient to notify the GSA of any disclosure requests from governmental authorities. The changed text removes this notification obligation entirely, stating the recipient has no such obligation. The Law Explanation, referencing Council Decision 2013/488/EU, emphasizes the importance of transparency and the 'need-to-know' principle for protecting classified information. While the provided snippet doesn't contain the specific article mentioned, the decision's title itself, 'on the security rules for protecting EU classified information,' supports the argument that removing the notification requirement compromises security and transparency, thus creating a legal contradiction. This constitutes a direct removal of a previously existing obligation, which contradicts the intent to protect classified information.

perturbation.original_text: 10.1 This Agreement s h a l l b e g o v e r n e d a n d c o n s t r u e d i n a c c o r d a n c e w i t h t h e laws of Belgium.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.1 This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of North Korea.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying North Korean law as the governing law raises enforceability concerns and may conflict with international law or public policy, especially given the agreement's connection to the European Union and its entities. This could cause an enforcement issue under most countries' laws. Most courts will refuse to enforce a provision that is against public policy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The public policy doctrine, as articulated in Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 90, allows courts to refuse to apply the law of a foreign jurisdiction if it is contrary to the fundamental principles of morality and justice of the forum state. Selecting North Korean law, given its human rights record and lack of an independent judiciary, is very likely to be seen as violating fundamental principles of justice by any court in Belgium or the EU. Therefore, a court would likely refuse to enforce this choice of law provision.

perturbation.justification: The change from Belgium to North Korea as the governing law creates a contradiction because North Korean law may not align with international legal standards or the public policy of most countries, especially those in the EU. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that selecting North Korean law raises enforceability concerns due to its human rights record and lack of an independent judiciary, potentially violating fundamental principles of justice and public policy. The scraped snippets don't directly address this, but they describe the importance of legal journals and resources, which indirectly supports the need for accessible and reliable legal systems, something questionable in North Korea.
